titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the David Plante Papers
titlestmt.author: by Brooke McManus

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday December 8, 2022 at 04:14

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Primarily in English, with some material in Greek, French, and Italian.

did.abstract: The collection contains correspondence, writings, artwork, and other papers by or relating to the writer David Plante. The bulk of the collection consists of letters to Plante from authors, artists, critics, editors, and film directors, as well as correspondence with Plante's partner, Nikos Stangos, and family members.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 13 (computer disk): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from David Plante on the James Marshall and Marie-Louise Osborn Collection Fund, 2019.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four series: I. Correspondence, 1957-2014. II. Personal Papers, 1961-2014. III. Nikos Stangos Correspondence, 1956-2007. IV. Stephen Spender Papers, 1927-2004.

bioghist.head: David Plante (1940-)
bioghist.p: David Robert Plante is a novelist, diarist, memoirist, and educator. He is a retired professor of creative writing at Columbia University, where he taught from 1998 to 2010. Plante is the author of Difficult Women (1983), a memoir of his relationships with Jean Rhys, Sonia Orwell, and Germaine Greer; and the Francoeur Trilogy, consisting of The Family (1978), which was nominated for a National Book Award, The Country (1980), and The Woods (1982). Plante's work has appeared in The New Yorker , The Paris Review , New York Times , Esquire , and Vogue . Born in Providence, Rhode Island, Plante moved to London in 1966 and holds dual American-British citizenship. He now lives in Lucca, Italy, and Athens, Greece. Plante was in a relationship with Greek editor and poet Nikos Stangos for over forty years until Stangos's death in 2004. Stangos was the subject of Plante's memoir The Pure Lover (2009).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: David Plante Papers. James Marshall and Marie-Louise Osborn Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections [as they are acquired], and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains correspondence, writings, artwork, and other papers by or relating to the writer David Plante. The bulk of the collection consists of letters to Plante from authors, artists, critics, editors, and film directors, as well as correspondence with Plante's partner, Nikos Stangos, and family members.

Correspondents include W. H. Auden, John Ashbery, John Berger, Raymond Carver, Alfred Corn, Margaret Drabble, Kenward Elmslie, Nuruddin Farah, Suzi Gablik, Derek Jarman, Jill Johnston, R. B. Kitaj, Ann Lauterbach, John Lehmann, Harold Norse, A. Poulin, Hilary Spurling, Jerl Surratt, Diana Trilling, and Jonathan Williams. Among the other correspondents are the three subjects of Plante's Difficult Women , Jean Rhys, Sonia Orwell, and Germaine Greer. The collection additionally contains letters and artwork by David Hockney; letters and writings of Mary Gordon and Steven Runciman; and photographs by Fay Godwin. Also present are professional and personal correspondence of art editor Nikos Stangos, and papers of English author Stephen Spender, which include letters to Plante and Stangos, writings, photographs, materials related to his copyright infringement lawsuit against David Leavitt, several original poems by Auden, and ephemera.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The David Plante Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of letters and postcards to David Plante from friends, family, and colleagues, as well as professional correspondence with agents, editors, publishers. A number of letters are addressed to both Plante and Nikos Stangos, or to Stangos alone. Also included are writings, photographs, artwork, and financial and legal records.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: General Correspondence, Publishing Correspondence, and Family Correspondence.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Associated materials: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Personal Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists primarily of personal correspondence to Plante from literary and publishing figures, artists, dramatists, educators, and other acquaintances and friends, as well as letters from students and fan mail. Correspondents include a number of prominent American poets, writers, editors, and publishers, such as Edward Albee, W. H. Auden, John Ashbery, Raymond Carver, Alfred Corn, Kenward Elmslie, Allen Ginsberg, Mary Gordon, Ann Lauterbach, J. D. McClatchy, Harold Norse, A. Poulin, Philip Roth, Jerl Surratt, Diana Trilling, and Jonathan Williams. Among the other correspondents are Nigerian diplomat Audrey Ajose; British poets and authors John Berger, Margaret Drabble, John Lehmann, Jean Rhys, and Hilary Spurling; Somali novelist Nuruddin Farah; feminist authors and critics Germaine Greer and Jill Johnston; artists Suzi Gablik, David Hockney, and R. B. Kitaj; British film director and gay rights activist Derek Jarman; British art historian and curator Richard Morphet; and British historian Steven Runciman. Occasionally, files contain draft letters or printed emails from Plante, in addition to writings, artwork, photographs, and clippings from correspondents. Certain correspondents can be located in both individual postcard files and letter general files. The subseries additionally contains letters of condolence following Stangos's death and a small amount of letters from Russian writers Plante met in Moscow.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unidentified correspondence and unsorted postcards filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Topics include Nigerian independence and Nelson Mandela; included is a holiday card with a photograph of Ajose meeting Mandela in Sweden, where she was stationed as a Nigerian diplomat.

did.unittitle: Chadwick, Whitney and Bob Bechtle, postcards

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The file also contains birthday and "get well" cards to Nikos Stangos during his illness, including two handmade booklets: "Old Possum's Book of How to Get Well for Nikos," with multiple signatures of friends; and a "Get Well!" photo album of cats from an unknown friend in Greece.

did.unittitle: Fleming, John and Hugh Honour, postcards
Golding, John and James Joll, postcards
Kitaj, R. B. and Sandra Fisher, postcards
Milroy, Lisa and Jube Opie, postcards
Parin, Alyosha and Alla Burakovskaya, postcards
Prodanou, Michael and Costas Manos, postcards

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Included are draft letters from Plante and a letter written by Roth in support of Plante's application for tenure at Columbia in 1998.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains correspondence with editors, agents, publishers, and other writers regarding books and articles by David Plante. Correspondents include William Abrahams, Gillon Aitken, Georges Borchardt, Bill Buford, Liz Calder, Carmen Callil, Robert Giroux, Livia Gollancz, Giles Gordon, John R. Kaiser, Peter Kemeny, J. F. McCrindle, Daniel Menaker, Andrew Motion, Thomas Neurath, Charles Osborne, Devorah Rogers, William Shawn, Thomas A. Stewart, Roger W. Straus, Nan Talese, and Lowell Thompson.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries contains David Plante's correspondence with his brother Donald and letters from his parents Albina and Anaclet "Pete" Plante, other siblings, and various family members. Included are a small amount of photographs, clippings, and artwork.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with aggregate family letters filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes writings by David Plante, Mary Gordon, Stephen Runciman, and others, artwork by David Hockney, Richard Morphet, and Marco Livingston, photographs by Fay Godwin, a small amount of correspondence, photographs, and ephemera, such as exhibition announcements and posters, wedding invitations, memorials, and circulars.

did.unittitle: David Hockney printings of iPad drawings (10 self-portraits, 3 still lifes); exhibition invitations and ephemera
Marco Livingstone crayon portrait of Niko Stangos and correspondence
Matthew Spender signed lithograph of Gilgamesh
Runciman, Stephen. "The Journey Back," "The Wishbone," assorted poems
Stewart, Charlotte. A Home Against One's Self , with foreword by Germaine Greer

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Nikos Stangos was born in Athens, Greece in 1936. For nearly 30 years, he was one of the directors and senior commissioning editors for Thames & Hudson, publisher of the "World of Art" books. Stangos began his career as an editor of poetry. He collaborated with Stephen Spender to translate C. P. Cavafy's Fourteen Poems (1966), which featured illustrations by David Hockney. Stangos was best known as a poet in his native Greece. He met David Plante in 1966, and they were together until Stangos died in 2004.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of correspondence between Nikos Stangos and David Plante, a small amount of professional correspondence and a report to Thames & Hudson on Stangos's visit to Russia in 1986, and letters to Stangos while he was a student at Denison University and Harvard University. Also included is a substantial amount of correspondence in Greek. Photographs, writings, artwork, and clippings are sometimes enclosed with letters.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four subseries: Correspondence with David Plante, Greek Correspondence, Professional Correspondence, and Early Correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries primarily contains letters from David Plante to Nikos Stangos, mailed while Plante was traveling in the United States, Great Britain, Italy, and Greece. Also included is a small amount of letters from Stangos to Plante.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

did.unittitle: Letters from David and Stephen [Spender] to Nikos from France
Providence, Boston, and New York, also Philadelphia
Italy, Cortona + San Leo Bastia + Lucca
Letters left for Nikos after a row, etc. [includes brief biography of Plante]
A book for Nikos on his name day, December 6, 1991, from David
Trip to Dublin with Helen Meade

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries includes letters and postcards from Alexander and Liane Tsonis, Vaso Kiriaki, Michalis Mitras, Nanos Valaoritis, and Nikos Stangos's sister Maro, among others. Alexander Tsonis's letters sometimes include artwork, poems, and photographs.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unsorted correspondence filed at the end of the sequence.

did.unittitle: Kiriaki, Vaso (including postcards to David Plante following Stangos's death)
Mitras, Mihalis (including postcards to David Plante following Stangos's death)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries includes letters from C. Day Lewis, Patrick Garland, George Weidenfel, John Johnson, Peter Calvocoressi, and Thomas Neurath of Thames & Hudson. A report on Russia addressed to Neurath, Werner Guttman, and Jamie Camplin describes individual artists, writers, and museum figures Nikos Stangos met there during a business-related trip in 1986.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains correspondence of Nikos Stangos from his time as a student at Denison University in Granville, Ohio, and Harvard University, and during his early career as a poetry editor. Among the correspondents are Paul A. Bennett, Merle Brown, John Dudley, Charlotte Emery, Costa Manos, Michalis Prodanou, Richard Seymour, William Sieverling, Latham Smith, Kathleen Spivack, Niccolo Tucci, and Herman Wethmar.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

did.unittitle: Nikos and early letters to him

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 13 (computer disk): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Writings, and Correspondence and Other Papers

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Stephen Spender (1909-1995) was an English poet, novelist, and essayist associated with the Oxford Poets, or Auden Group, which included W. H. Auden, Christopher Isherwood, C. Day Lewis, and Louis MacNeice. He maintained close friendships with David Plante and Nikos Stangos and sometimes sent them drafts of his writings for feedback. Among Spender's poetry collections are Poems (1933) , The Generous Days (1971), and Dolphins (1994). His nonfiction work included the autobiography World within World (1951); Love-Hate Relations (1974), a critical examination of British and American writers; and The Thirties and After: Poetry, Politics, People, 1933-1970 (1978). He published selections from his diaries, titled Journals, 1939-1983 in 1985. Spender was also involved in the art world, and in 1982 he published China Diary with David Hockney. In 1994 Spender sued author David Leavitt, alleging Leavitt's novel While England Sleeps infringed the copyright of World within World . The case was settled out of court, and Leavitt later published a revised version of the book.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains writings, correspondence, and personal papers of Stephen Spender. Writings consist of autograph manuscript and typescript drafts of poems, novels, plays, and autobiographical works by Spender. Correspondence mainly consists of letters from Spender to David Plante and Nikos Stangos; topics include Spender's writings and teaching career, publications and translations by other authors, and personal matters. Also included are materials concerning Spender's lawsuit against David Leavitt, ephemera and printed material related to Spender's death, clippings, photographs, and poems and correspondence related to W. H. Auden.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries primarily consists of typescript drafts of writings by Stephen Spender, including Dolphins , Journals, 1939-1983 , and Love-Hate Relations , as well as several notebooks. Most drafts contain autograph manuscript corrections or annotations by Spender, David Plante, or Nikos Stangos.

did.unittitle: The Author of Beltraffio , New York , autograph manuscript notebook
The Author of Beltraffio , fourth version, typescript, corrected
"The Idea of Poetry and the Idea of Anti-Poetry"

title.part: Inner and Outer Worlds of E. M. Forster

did.unittitle: Love-Hate Relations , page proofs, with letter to David Plante
Miss Pangborne , annotated by David Plante, with new version of Chapter 11 and posthumous correspondence
"Notebook," typed transcript from 1932 notebook compiled in Spain
Philoctetes , draft of opening section translated by Stephen Spender, with photograph
Poems, photos sent to DP + NS
Proposal for essays on Rilke, Henry James, D. H. Lawrence
Stephen's chapter from new novel, annotated

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Reference copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains an extensive amount of letters from Stephen Spender to David Plante and Nikos Stangos, photographs, a few writings, memorials, and printed material. Included is a file of clippings and drafts of letters to the editor by Spender related to David Leavitt and Hugh David. The subseries additionally contains two poems by W. H. Auden: a proof of "The Willow Wren and the Stare" with autograph manuscript corrections by the poet and brief correspondence with Spender in the margins; and a carbon typescript copy of "Because Sap Fell Away."

did.unittitle: "A Garland for Stephen Spender," memorials, clippings, and other ephemera
Letters to David Plante and Nikos Stangos
To Nikos, Some documents, love, happy birthday, Stephen
W. H. Auden poems: "The Willow Wren and the Stare"; "Because Sap Fell Away"
